topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor stared at the printed memo, the ink blurring slightly under her gaze. She had spent the last three weeks meticulously crafting her performance reviews, anticipating the inevitable moment when the leadership would acknowledge her dedication. Then, the announcement came: Mark was the chosen candidate for the Senior Manager role. Eleanor felt a strange, hollow sensation settle in her chest, like a sudden drop in air pressure. She slowly folded the paper, the crisp edges crinkling under her fingertips. A faint tremor ran through her hand as she picked up her coffee mug, the warmth doing little to soothe the unexpected chill that had taken root. "Well," she managed, the sound of her own voice thin and reedy, "that certainly shifts the landscape."

[story 2]

Liam leaned back in his ergonomic chair, his jaw slackening as he read the internal newsletter. His eyes scanned the text, skipping lines, then returning to the highlighted section detailing the promotion to Director. His initial reaction was a slow, almost physical freeze. He blinked several times, trying to recalibrate his vision to process the information. A dull throb began behind his temples. He let out a breath he hadn't realized he was holding, a sound that was more of an involuntary expulsion of air than a conscious sigh. He looked across the office at Sarah, who was already smiling, and felt a peculiar tightness around his throat. He cleared his throat, the sound surprisingly loud in the quiet cubicle, and simply nodded, the movement stiff.

[story 3]

From the moment the email landed in David’s inbox, a knot tightened in his stomach. He reread the subject line three times, a frantic loop of disbelief playing out behind his eyes. He set his mouse down with deliberate slowness, the click of the mouse echoing unnaturally loud in the open-plan workspace. When he finally looked up, his gaze landed on the whiteboard where the promotion slots were listed. His breathing hitched, shallow and quick. He pushed his chair back an inch, the scrape of the wheels against the linoleum sounding like a declaration. He didn't speak immediately; instead, he just stared at the ceiling tiles, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of his desk, the world around him seeming momentarily muted and distant.
